title: Primitives as Fixed Points of Reflexive Decomposition
subtitle: When a system decomposes itself, what survives unchanged ARE the primitives

children.text: What if you don't need to discover primitives by analyzing data? What if you can derive them from pure self-reference? Apply a decomposition operator to ITSELF — whatever survives unchanged is, by definition, primitive. This is Lawvere's fixed-point theorem (1969) applied to cognitive science.

children.title: The Problem: How Do You Know When You've Hit Bottom?

children.text: Every decomposition system faces the same question: when do you stop? Schank decomposed 'give' into ATRANS (abstract transfer of possession). But why is ATRANS primitive? What makes it irreducible?

The standard answer is unsatisfying: 'because Schank said so' or 'because further decomposition doesn't help.' But there's a rigorous mathematical answer that nobody has applied to cognitive primitives.

items.definition: Abstract transfer — change of possession or control (give, take, buy)
Physical transfer — movement through space (go, walk, fly)
Mental transfer — communication of information (tell, ask, read)
Application of physical force (push, hit, throw)
Movement of a body part (wave, kick, nod)
Taking something inside an organism (eat, drink, breathe)
Removing something from inside an organism (exhale, spit, cry)

children.text: In 1969, F. William Lawvere proved a stunning generalization: Cantor's diagonal argument, Gödel's incompleteness theorem, Turing's halting problem, and Russell's paradox are ALL instances of the same abstract pattern.
If there exists a surjective map from A to the function space B^A (i.e., A can 'index' all transformations of B), then every endomorphism f: B → B has a fixed point — some b ∈ B where f(b) = b.

Translation for our purposes: if your decomposition system is powerful enough to decompose ANYTHING (including itself), then there MUST exist elements that are invariant under decomposition. Those fixed points are your primitives.
Apply each primitive to itself:
• ATRANS(ATRANS) = 'Transfer the concept of transferring' = still ATRANS ✓ (fixed point)
• PTRANS(PTRANS) = 'Physically move the concept of physical movement' = still PTRANS ✓ (fixed point)
• MTRANS(MTRANS) = 'Communicate about communication' = still MTRANS ✓ (fixed point)
• But 'give'(give) = 'Transfer possession of giving' = ATRANS(give) = ATRANS ✗ (NOT a fixed point — decomposes further)

A primitive is defined by the property that it cannot be further decomposed by the decomposition operation. Mathematically: p is primitive iff D(p) = {p}, where D is the decomposition functor. This is literally the definition of a fixed point of D.

Lawvere's theorem guarantees these exist whenever D is powerful enough to be applied to its own domain. Since Schankian decomposition applies to ALL events (including the event of decomposing), fixed points must exist. The primitives aren't chosen — they're forced into existence by the reflexivity of the system.

children.title: From Theory to Construction: HRR Self-Improvement

children.text: This isn't just philosophy. We built a system that proves it constructively. The ontology recursive improver uses four operations: bind, unbind, similarity, and superpose. When it analyzes its own failures, it uses these SAME operations on ITSELF.
The system encodes its own failures as HRR vectors:
failure_vector = bind(FAILURE_MODE, 'verb_wrong') ⊛ bind(SOURCE, 'Q53')
Then clusters failures using the SAME similarity operation it uses for reasoning:
similarity(failure_A.vector, failure_B.vector) → geometric grouping
Then extracts the pattern using the SAME unbind it uses to extract semantic roles:
unbind(FAILURE_MODE, cluster_centroid) → 'verb extraction is the problem'
The operations that CANNOT be further simplified by this process — bind, unbind, similarity, superpose — are the computational fixed points. They ARE the primitives of this system.

children.title: Like a Microscope Looking at Itself

children.text: Imagine a microscope powerful enough to examine its own lenses. It could analyze every component — except the fundamental optical laws that make microscopy possible. Those laws are the 'fixed points' of microscopic examination: they enable the analysis but cannot themselves be further analyzed by the same method. That's what primitives are: the operations that enable decomposition but cannot themselves be decomposed.

children.title: Can You Find the Fixed Points?
children.description: Given a set of operations, which ones survive self-application? Click each operation to 'apply it to itself' and see if it reduces further or stays the same.

operations.name: ATRANS
operations.explanation: ATRANS applied to itself is still abstract transfer — it's irreducible.

operations.name: give
operations.explanation: 'Give' decomposes into ATRANS + a possession object. Not primitive.

operations.name: PTRANS
operations.explanation: PTRANS applied to itself is still physical transfer — irreducible.

operations.name: walk
operations.explanation: 'Walk' decomposes into PTRANS + an instrument specification. Not primitive.

operations.name: bind
operations.explanation: The HRR bind operation applied to itself is still binding — it's a computational primitive.

operations.name: encode
operations.explanation: 'Encode' decomposes into bind + normalize + hash. Not primitive.

operations.name: similarity
operations.explanation: The cosine similarity operation is irreducible — it IS the measurement.

operations.name: match
operations.explanation: 'Match' decomposes into similarity + threshold + selection. Not primitive.

children.text: This gives us a constructive algorithm for discovering primitives in ANY domain — no corpus analysis, no clustering, no training:
1. Build a decomposition system for your domain
2. Make it reflexive (capable of analyzing its own operations)
3. Apply it to its own vocabulary until convergence
4. Whatever remains unchanged = your primitives
Lawvere guarantees this terminates (fixed points must exist).

This reframes primitive discovery from an empirical question ('what patterns recur in data?') to a structural one ('what survives self-reference?'). The two approaches should converge — and when they do, that's strong evidence the primitives are real, not arbitrary.
Lawvere's insight unifies ALL diagonal/fixed-point arguments:

items.definition: The set of subsets of S can't be mapped onto by S. Fixed point: the diagonal set.
Sufficiently strong systems can encode self-reference. Fixed point: the unprovable-but-true statement.
No program can decide all programs. Fixed point: the self-referential program.
A sufficiently general decomposition system has irreducible elements. Fixed point: the primitives themselves.

children.text: Chemical elements are 'fixed points' of nuclear decomposition: you can break molecules into atoms, but atoms of a given element always remain that element under chemical processes. It took the periodic table to organize them, but their existence was forced by the mathematics of stability — not discovered empirically first. Similarly, Schankian primitives may be derivable from the structure of self-referential decomposition before any corpus analysis.

questions.question: What does Lawvere's fixed-point theorem guarantee about decomposition systems?
options: That they always terminate in finite steps
That reflexive systems must have elements invariant under self-application
That all decompositions produce exactly 11 primitives
That primitives are always linguistically motivated
questions.explanation: Lawvere's theorem says: if a system is powerful enough to index all its own transformations (reflexive), then every endomorphism has a fixed point — an element that maps to itself. For decomposition, these fixed points are the irreducible primitives.

questions.question: Why does ATRANS(ATRANS) = ATRANS make it a 'fixed point'?
options: Because Schank defined it that way
Because transfer is the most common verb in English
Because applying the decomposition to ATRANS returns ATRANS unchanged
Because ATRANS has the fewest semantic features
questions.explanation: A fixed point is defined as f(x) = x. When you 'decompose' ATRANS by applying the concept to itself, you get ATRANS back — it doesn't reduce to anything simpler. That's exactly what 'primitive' means: irreducible under the decomposition operation.

questions.question: How does the HRR recursive improver demonstrate fixed points constructively?
options: It trains a neural network to classify primitives
It uses bind/unbind/similarity to analyze itself, and those operations are the ones that can't be further simplified by the same process
It counts how many times each operation is called
It compares word frequencies in a corpus
questions.explanation: The system uses bind, unbind, similarity, and superpose to analyze its own failures. These four operations ARE the computational fixed points — they enable the self-analysis but cannot themselves be further decomposed by it. They're the system's own primitives, discovered constructively.

children.text: Lawvere's Fixed-Point Theorem (nLab)
— The original categorical generalization
Soto-Andrade & Varela 1984
— Extension to autonomous (self-referential) systems
Yanofsky 2003 — A Universal Approach to Self-Referential Paradoxes
— Shows Cantor, Gödel, Turing, Russell all follow from one scheme
Mudholkar et al. 2025 — Reflexive Category Theory
— Unified framework for self-reference in mathematics and CS
